items.title: Wound contamination
items.contentHtml:
Degrees of wound contamination

  • The Gustilo-Anderson classification subdivides open fractures according to the degree of contamination and soft tissue damage, determining the risk of infection and therapeutic conduct.
  • Degree of contamination: it is evaluated by the mechanism of injury (high energy, environmental, agricultural) and the presence of foreign bodies; The greater the contamination, the greater the risk of infection and the need for aggressive debridement.
  • Severely contaminated wounds (with dirt, feces, organic material) are automatically classified as Type III, regardless of size, due to the high risk of polymicrobial infection.
  • The time elapsed since the injury (>8 hours) increases bacterial contamination, but does not modify the Gustilo-Anderson classification per se, although it does accelerate the indication for debridement and prophylactic antibiotics.

items.foco: Type I: clean wound less than 1 cm
items.title: Type I: clean wound < 1 cm
items.contentHtml:
Type I: clean wound less than 1 cm

  • Gustilo-Anderson Type I: clean wound, less than 1 cm in length, with minimal soft tissue damage, without significant contamination or crushing.
  • The fracture is usually simple, with minimal or absent comminution, and the mechanism is low energy (e.g. fall from own height).
  • Management: local debridement, abundant washing, delayed primary or primary closure if the wound is clean, and prophylactic antibiotic therapy with first-generation cephalosporin (cefazolin 1-2 g intravenously) for 24 hours.
  • Trap examination: does not require systematic wound culture; the risk of infection is low (<2%) if adequate debridement and antibiotic prophylaxis are performed.

items.foco: Type II: wound greater than 1 cm without extensive damage
items.title: Type II: wound > 1 cm without extensive damage
items.contentHtml:
Type II: wound greater than 1 cm without extensive damage

  • Gustilo-Anderson Type II: wound greater than 1 cm, with moderate soft tissue damage, without loss of bone coverage, without extensive avulsion or severe crushing.
  • Contamination is moderate, and the fracture may present mild to moderate comminution, but the periosteum and adjacent muscle are viable.
  • Management: surgical debridement, pressure saline lavage, and prophylactic antibiotic therapy with first-generation cephalosporin (cefazolin 1-2 g intravenously) plus an aminoglycoside (gentamicin 5 mg/kg/day) if there is significant contamination, for 24-48 hours.
  • Trap examination: unlike Type I, here the risk of infection is intermediate (2-10%) and stable internal fixation is recommended if the wound allows adequate coverage after debridement.

items.title: Type IIIA: adequate bone coverage
items.contentHtml:
Type IIIA: adequate bone coverage

  • Gustilo-Anderson Type IIIA: high-energy open fracture with wound >10 cm, but with sufficient soft tissue coverage to close the wound without the need for flaps; periosteum and muscle cover the bone.
  • Includes fractures with segmental comminution, but without extensive loss of skin or muscle; exposed bone may be covered with viable tissue.
  • Management: aggressive surgical debridement, pressure lavage, prophylactic antibiotic therapy with first-generation cephalosporin (cefazolin) plus aminoglycoside (gentamicin), and consider penicillin if anaerobic contamination (e.g. soil).
  • Trap examination: the risk of infection is high (10-20%), and temporary external fixation is often preferred over definitive internal fixation until the wound is clean.

items.foco: Type IIIB: loss of bone coverage
items.title: Type IIIB: loss of bone coverage
items.contentHtml:
Type IIIB: loss of bone coverage

  • Gustilo-Anderson Type IIIB: open fracture with extensive soft tissue loss, bone exposure requiring flap coverage (local or free) to achieve closure; the bone is denuded of periosteum.
  • Frequently associated with massive contamination, severe muscle injury and high risk of infection and osteomyelitis.
  • Management: repeated surgical debridement (every 24-48 hours) until the wound is clean, prophylactic antibiotic therapy with first-generation cephalosporin + aminoglycoside + penicillin (if anaerobic contamination), and external fixation temporary; the flap must be performed within the first 7 days.
  • Trap examination: the risk of infection is very high (20-50%); Wound culture is not systematic to decide antibiotics, but it is useful if there is suspicion of resistance.

items.foco: Type IIIC: vascular injury requiring repair
items.title: Type IIIC: vascular injury requiring repair
items.contentHtml:
Type IIIC: vascular injury requiring repair

  • Gustilo-Anderson Type IIIC: open fracture with arterial injury that compromises distal perfusion and requires urgent vascular surgical repair, regardless of soft tissue damage.
  • Distal ischemia (>6 hours) increases the risk of amputation; ischemia time is critical for limb viability.
  • Management: hemorrhage control, vascular repair (bypass or suture) within 6 hours, temporary external fixation, and broad-spectrum prophylactic antibiotic therapy (cephalosporin + aminoglycoside + penicillin).
  • Trap examination: amputation is indicated in Type IIIC with irreversible ischemia (>6-8 hours) or unrecoverable vascular injury; MESS (Mangled Extremity Severity Score) score >7 predicts amputation.

items.foco: Prophylactic antibiotic according to fracture type
items.title: Prophylactic antibiotic according to type
items.contentHtml:
Prophylactic antibiotic according to type of fracture

  • Type I: cefazolin 1-2 g intravenously every 8 hours for 24 hours (covers gram-positive patients).
  • Type II: cefazolin plus gentamicin 5 mg/kg/day intravenous (covers gram-negative) for 24-48 hours.
  • Type III (A, B, C): cefazolin + gentamicin + penicillin G 2-4 million units intravenously every 4 hours (covers anaerobes, especially Clostridium) for 48-72 hours or until the wound is clean.
  • Trap exam: topical antibiotic is not recommended; Prophylaxis should be started as soon as possible (ideally within 3 hours of injury) and should not be continued for more than 72 hours unless there are signs of infection.

items.title: Initial debridement in 6-8 hours
items.contentHtml:
Initial surgical debridement in 6-8 hours

  • Surgical debridement should be performed within the first 6-8 hours of injury to reduce bacterial load and prevent infection; This is the gold standard in open fractures.
  • It includes excision of all devitalized tissue (skin, muscle, bone), pressure saline lavage (9 liters or more in Type III), and hemostasis.
  • In Type III fractures, a second debridement is recommended after 24-48 hours to reevaluate tissue viability.
  • Trap examination: Initial debridement should not be delayed by imaging studies; Prophylactic antibiotic therapy is not a substitute for complete surgical debridement.

items.title: Unsystematic wound culture
items.contentHtml:
Wound culture is not systematic

  • Routine wound culture is not recommended at the time of initial debridement, as it has a low correlation with subsequent infection and may lead to unnecessary antibiotic treatment.
  • Culture is indicated only if there are clinical signs of infection (pus, fever, progressive erythema) or in wounds with suspected resistant pathogens (e.g. gunshot wounds, contamination fecal).
  • In case of confirmed infection, deep cultures (bone, bone tissue) are taken to guide targeted antibiotic therapy.
  • Trap examination: superficial culture of the wound is not reliable; The empirical antibiotic decision is based on the Gustilo-Anderson classification, not on the culture result.

items.foco: External fixation in severe open fractures
items.title: External fixation in severe fractures
items.contentHtml:
External fixation in severe open fractures

  • External fixation is the method of choice in Type IIIB and IIIC open fractures and in those with severe contamination or hemodynamic instability, as part of orthopedic damage control.
  • It allows rapid stabilization, access for serial debridements and evaluation of soft tissues, minimizing additional damage to the periosteum.
  • Definitive internal fixation (intramedullary nail or plate) is deferred until the wound is clean and without signs of infection (generally 7-14 days).
  • Trap examination: in Type I and II fractures with a clean wound, primary internal fixation is acceptable if adequate debridement and antibiotic prophylaxis are performed.

items.foco: Amputation indicated in Type IIIC with ischemia
items.title: Type IIIC amputation with ischemia
items.contentHtml:
Amputation indicated in Type IIIC with ischemia

  • Amputation is indicated in Type IIIC open fractures when ischemia is irreversible (ischemia time >6-8 hours, absence of distal Doppler flow) or when vascular repair is not possible due to extensive damage.
  • The Mangled Extremity Severity Score (MESS) is a prognostic tool: score >7 predicts high probability of amputation; includes age, shock, ischemia, and bone/soft tissue injury.
  • Other criteria: posterior tibial nerve injury, massive muscle destruction, fecal or salt water contamination.
  • Trap examination: the amputation decision is not based solely on the Gustilo-Anderson classification; multidisciplinary evaluation is required (orthopedic, vascular, intensivist surgeon).

items.foco: Gustilo-Anderson does not include gunshot fractures
items.title: Exclusion of gunshot fractures
items.contentHtml:
Gustilo-Anderson does not include gunshot fractures

  • The Gustilo-Anderson classification was designed for open fractures due to high-energy trauma (traffic accidents, falls) and does not include gunshot fractures, which have different mechanism, contamination and injury pattern.
  • Firearm fractures are classified according to the type of projectile (low vs. high speed) and contamination by gunpowder, metals and environmental germs; Its management follows specific guidelines (debridement, antibiotic depending on the wound).
  • In low-velocity firearm wounds (e.g. pistol), management can be similar to a closed fracture if there is no contamination; in high velocity (rifle) they are treated as Type III for extensive damage.
  • Cheating trap: applying the Gustilo-Anderson classification to a gunshot fracture is a conceptual error; Wound classification based on weapon and tissue damage should be used.
